steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Besterman and Pace found benthic chlorophyll concentrations did not negatively correlate with macroalgal proliferation, which contradicts the expected result. They then noted that MPB can respond to low light by producing extra chlorophyll. The blank must state the conclusion they drew from that observation about what this compensation means for their measurements.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'researchers ought to account for the possibility that because MPB have the capacity to compensate for reduced sunlight availability, benthic chlorophyll concentrations may not always be a reliable indicator of MPB biomass.': This directly follows from the stated reasoning: if MPB boost chlorophyll under low light, elevated chlorophyll can mask declining biomass, making the proxy unreliable. ✓
  • Choice B — 'although their finding was inconsistent with that of García-Robledo and Corzo, this discrepancy was not attributable to the ability of MPB to accelerate chlorophyll production...': The passage says the compensation mechanism is exactly what Besterman and Pace noted as an explanation. This choice denies the mechanism they invoked. ✗
  • Choice C — 'there is likely a larger trend in MPB biomass that is related to macroalgal presence but unrelated to light conditions.': The passage focuses on light conditions as the driver of chlorophyll compensation. Invoking a trend unrelated to light contradicts the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — 'the effect of macroalgal concentrations on MPB abundance...was not observed in Curlew Bay and other Virginia sites because low-light conditions likely are not generalizable across the sites.': The passage does not suggest low-light conditions are site-specific or non-generalizable; it attributes the unexpected result to the compensation mechanism, not to site variation. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
